sections.title: Genetics & Features
sections.content:
**Mandibulofacial Dysostosis**

**Genetics:**
• TCOF1 gene (most common) - 90%
• POLR1C, POLR1D genes
• Autosomal dominant (60% de novo)
• Variable expressivity

**Classic Features:**
• Malar hypoplasia (underdeveloped cheekbones)
• Downslanting palpebral fissures
• Coloboma of lower eyelid
• Micrognathia/retrognathia
• Ear anomalies (microtia, atresia)
• Conductive hearing loss

**Intelligence:** Usually normal

sections.title: Clinical Manifestations
sections.content:
**Craniofacial:**
• Hypoplastic zygomas and mandible
• Cleft palate (30%)
• Choanal atresia/stenosis

**Eyes:**
• Downslanting palpebral fissures
• Lower lid coloboma
• Absent lower lashes

**Ears:**
• Microtia to anotia
• External auditory canal atresia
• Ossicular anomalies
• Conductive hearing loss (bilateral)

**Airway:**
• Micrognathia with glossoptosis
• Can have severe obstruction (like PRS)
• Difficult intubation

sections.title: Management
sections.content:
**Multidisciplinary Team Essential**

**Airway:**
• Assess at birth
• May need prone positioning, NPA
• Some require tracheostomy
• Mandibular distraction

**Hearing:**
• Early amplification (BAHA)
• Critical for speech development
• Usually not surgical candidates for atresia repair

**Surgical Timeline:**
• Infancy: Airway management, feeding
• 2-3 years: Soft tissue procedures
• 5-7 years: Orbital/malar reconstruction
• Teens: Orthognathic surgery, rhinoplasty
• Adult: Final refinements

**Psychosocial:**
• Support for appearance differences
• Normal intelligence = normal expectations

reviewQuestions.question: A newborn has malar hypoplasia, downslanting palpebral fissures, and bilateral microtia. The parents appear unaffected. What is the inheritance pattern and what percentage of cases are de novo?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Treacher Collins syndrome is autosomal dominant with approximately 60% of cases arising from de novo mutations. Parents may appear unaffected due to variable expressivity or it may be a new mutation.
